verses.fhb_text: Blow a shofar in Zion; sound the alarm on my holy mountain! Let all who live in the land tremble, for the day of the LORD is coming; it is near.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Opening battle-call announcing the day of the LORD; the speaker shifts to YHWH ("my holy mountain").

Renderings:
• "shofar" — preserved transliteration; the ram's horn alarm specific to Israel's worship
• "the day of the LORD" — fixed title preserved per FHB-1.2-1 resolution order
• "my holy mountain" — first-person possessive marks YHWH as speaker; preserved as natural English
• Semicolon between the two ki-clauses — preserves Hebrew parallelism in modern punctuation per FHB-9.4-2

verses.fhb_text: A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness. Like dawn spreading across the mountains, a great and mighty army advances. Their like has never been before, nor will be again, even through the years of generation upon generation.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Locust/army dual imagery introduced — "great and mighty army" works for both insects and soldiers per FHB-5.11-1.

Renderings:
• "a great and mighty army advances" — am rav we'atzum carries dual force; "advances" works as both insect-swarm motion and military advance
• "Like dawn spreading" — following kashachar reading (NIV/NLT consensus per FHB-8.4-1)

verses.fhb_text: Before them fire devours; behind them flames burn. The land before them is like the garden of Eden; behind them, a desolate wilderness. Nothing escapes them.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Eden-vs-wilderness contrast intensifies the destruction.

Renderings:
• Semicolons preserve the Hebrew's parallel before/behind structure per FHB-9.4-2
• "Nothing escapes them" — separate sentence avoids em-dash overuse per FHB-9.5-1

verses.fhb_text: Their appearance is like the appearance of horses; like war-horses, they charge.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Locust-army comparison continues; "charge" is a vivid action verb working for both insects and cavalry.

Renderings:
• "war-horses" — parashim (cavalry/horsemen) as war-mounts preserves military force
• "they charge" — yerutsun rendered with vivid action verb per FHB-7.8-1

verses.fhb_text: Like the rumble of chariots they leap on the mountaintops, like the crackle of fire devouring stubble, like a mighty army arrayed for battle.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Three parallel similes intensify the locust-army's terror.

Renderings:
• "rumble" / "crackle" — concrete English sound-words for the Hebrew qol (sound)
• "leap on the mountaintops" — yeraqedun preserves both grasshopper-leap and chariot-bounce

verses.fhb_text: At their advance peoples writhe in anguish; every face grows pale.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Universal terror response.

Renderings:
• "writhe in anguish" — yachilu carries the labor-pain force; intensifies beyond plain "tremble"
• "every face grows pale" — following NIV/NLT/ESV consensus on the disputed pa'rur per FHB-8.4-1

verses.fhb_text: Like warriors they charge; like soldiers they scale walls. Each marches in line; they do not break ranks.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Military discipline of the locust-army.

Renderings:
• "scale walls" — ya'alu chomah; vivid military verb per FHB-7.8-1
• "do not break ranks" — lo ye'abetun orchotam, idiomatic for orderly march

verses.fhb_text: They do not jostle one another; each keeps to his lane. They plunge through the weapons and are not stopped.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Unbreakable formation; weapons cannot halt them.

Renderings:
• "plunge through" — vivid action verb for the difficult uv'ad hashelach yipolu phrase, following NIV/ESV consensus per FHB-8.4-1
• "each keeps to his lane" — gever bimsilato yelekun, modern English idiom

verses.fhb_text: They rush upon the city; they run on the wall. They climb into houses; they enter through windows like a thief.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Total breach — city, wall, houses, windows.

Renderings:
• "rush upon" / "run" / "climb" / "enter" — vivid sequential verbs for yashoqu/yerutsun/ya'alu/yavo'u per FHB-7.8-1
• "like a thief" — preserves the simile of stealth invasion

verses.fhb_text: Before them the earth quakes; the heavens tremble. The sun and moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Cosmic disturbance — Day-of-the-LORD imagery.

Renderings:
• "the heavens" — plural preserved per FHB-7.9-1
• "withdraw their shining" — asfu nogham, the stars gathering back their light

verses.fhb_text: Our Father thunders before his army, for his camp is vast; mighty is the one who carries out his word. Great is the day of the LORD, and dreadful. Who can endure it?
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: YHWH himself leads the army of judgment; the day of the LORD is preserved as fixed title.

Renderings:
• "Our Father thunders" — YHWH natan qolo (gave his voice) → vivid action verb per FHB-7.8-1; YHWH → our Father since this is covenant judgment on his own people per FHB-6.2.1-1
• "the day of the LORD" — fixed title retained per FHB-1.2-1 resolution order (overrides Father-rendering for this technical phrase)
• Pronoun shift to "his" after establishing "our Father" per FHB-9.2-1

Hebrew/Greek: natan qolo (gave his voice) → "thunders"

verses.fhb_text: "Even now," declares our Father, "return to me with all your heart, with fasting, weeping, and mourning."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The chapter's filial pivot — the Father directly calls his people home.

Renderings:
• "declares our Father" — ne'um YHWH; the Father's own self-attestation in a heart-call
• "return to me with all your heart" — shuvu adai bekol levavkhem; preserves the literal Hebrew, which is already the most intimate possible call
• Heart-language carries the force without amplification — the verse already speaks heart-to-heart
verses.footnote: The Father himself is calling. "Return to me with all your heart" is not a demand for ritual but our Father's longing for his children's hearts to come home.

verses.fhb_text: Rend your hearts, not your garments. Return to our Father, for he is gracious and tender-hearted, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love, and he relents from sending disaster.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The covenant formula (Exod 34:6) lands on the Father's heart-character.

Renderings:
• "tender-hearted" — rachum (compassionate, from racham/womb-love) amplified with heart-language per FHB-5.6.2-1 to surface the Father's tenderness rather than a clinical adjective
• "abounding in steadfast love" — rav-chesed; preserved standard rendering
• "he relents from sending disaster" — niham al-hara'ah; the Father's heart genuinely turns

Hebrew/Greek: rachum (womb-love compassion) → "tender-hearted"

verses.fhb_text: Who knows? He may turn and relent, and leave a blessing behind him, a grain offering and a drink offering for our Father.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Hope of restored worship.

Renderings:
• "a grain offering and a drink offering for our Father" — minchah vanesech laYHWH eloheikhem; YHWH-elohim collapsed to "our Father" per filial context
• Comma instead of em dash for the appositional clarification per FHB-9.5-1

verses.fhb_text: Blow a shofar in Zion; consecrate a fast; call a sacred assembly.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Solemn assembly call — sober imperatives, not battle-cry; period rather than exclamation per FHB-9.15-1.

verses.fhb_text: Gather the people; consecrate the assembly; bring together the elders; gather the children, even those nursing at the breast. Let the bridegroom come out from his chamber, and the bride from her bridal canopy.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Total participation — every age, even those who would normally be exempt.

Renderings:
• "those nursing at the breast" — yoneqei shadayim; modern English equivalent
• "bridal canopy" — chuppah; modern English transliteration is now common in English

verses.fhb_text: Between the porch and the altar let the priests, the ministers of our Father, weep and say: "Spare your people, our Father, and do not make your inheritance a reproach, a byword among the nations. Why should the peoples say, 'Where is their God?'"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Priestly intercession with embedded pagan-mockery quote.

Renderings:
• "the ministers of our Father" — mesharetei YHWH; priests serve YHWH as Father in covenant relationship
• "your inheritance" — nachalah; explicit filial marker (inheritance) per FHB-3.1-1
• "Where is their God?" — speaker-context exception per FHB-1.2-1: the quoted speakers are pagan nations, so divine name renders generically as "their God," not "their Father"

verses.fhb_text: Then our Father became zealous for his land, and his heart went out to his people.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The pivot — the Father's heart turns toward his people.

Renderings:
• "his heart went out to his people" — vayachmol al-amo (chamal/spare-pity) rendered with heart-language per FHB-5.6.2-1; "had compassion" softened to the relational warmth FHB voice carries
• "zealous" — vaykane, the protective jealousy of a Father for what is his

Hebrew/Greek: vayachmol (had pity/spared) → "his heart went out"

verses.fhb_text: Our Father replied to his people: "Look, I am sending you grain, new wine, and oil, and you will be satisfied. Never again will I make you a reproach among the nations.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father's promise of restoration begins.

Renderings:
• "replied" — vaya'an YHWH vayo'mer (answered and said); "answered" reserved for direct question-response per FHB-9.14-1; here the Father responds to a prayer/cry, so "replied" fits
• "new wine" — tirosh; preserved per FHB-4.5.1-1 (tirosh = new wine, not asis/sweet wine)

Hebrew/Greek: tirosh → "new wine"

verses.fhb_text: I will drive the northern army far from you, banishing it to a parched and desolate land. Its front will face the eastern sea, and its rear the western sea. Its stench will rise; its foul smell will rise, for it has done great things."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The northern locust-army driven into the wilderness; closing of YHWH's first speech.

Renderings:
• "the northern army" — ha-tzefoni; preserves the military-locust dual force
• Em dashes avoided per FHB-9.5-1 by restructuring into separate sentences

verses.fhb_text: Do not fear, O land! Rejoice and be glad, for our Father has done great things.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Prophet announces restoration — direct address to the land.

Renderings:
• Exclamation justified by direct vocative address ("O land!") per FHB-9.15-1
• "our Father has done great things" — YHWH higdil la'asot; renders the same verbal phrase from v.20 (where the locust-army "did great things" in destruction); the Father reverses the destruction with his own "great things"

verses.fhb_text: Do not fear, beasts of the field, for the pastures of the wilderness are green. The trees bear their fruit; the fig tree and the vine yield their riches.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Reversal of Joel 1's destruction — pastures green, trees fruitful again.

Renderings:
• "are green" — dasheu; modern English equivalent for the pastures coming back to life
• "yield their riches" — natnu cheilam; the fig and vine producing again

verses.fhb_text: Children of Zion, rejoice and be glad in our Father, your God, for he has given you the early rain in righteousness; he sends abundant rain, both early and latter, as in former times.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Explicit filial marker — "children of Zion" anchors the chapter's filial context per FHB-3.1-1.

Renderings:
• "Children of Zion" — bnei Tziyon; explicit filial address; the Father's people named as children
• "our Father, your God" — YHWH eloheikhem; preserves the relational double-naming
• "the early rain in righteousness" — moreh litzdaqah; preserves the literal sense (NIV reading per FHB-8.4-1)
• Pronoun shift to "he sends" after establishing "our Father" per FHB-9.2-1

Hebrew/Greek: bnei Tziyon (sons of Zion) → "children of Zion"

verses.fhb_text: The threshing floors will be full of grain; the vats will overflow with new wine and oil.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Harvest abundance restored.

Renderings:
• "new wine" — tirosh per FHB-4.5.1-1

Hebrew/Greek: tirosh → "new wine"

verses.fhb_text: "I will repay you for the years that the swarming locust ate — the young locust, the destroying locust, and the cutting locust — my great army that I sent against you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father claims the locust devastation as his own discipline; pledges full restoration.

Renderings:
• Four locust names preserved per FHB-5.11-1 — arbeh (swarming), yeleq (young), chasil (destroying), gazam (cutting)
• Em-dash pair earns its place per FHB-9.8-1 — sets off the appositional listing of three additional locust names from "swarming locust" and from "my great army"

Hebrew/Greek: arbeh / yeleq / chasil / gazam → four named locusts preserved

verses.fhb_text: You will eat in plenty and be satisfied, and you will praise the name of our Father, your God, who has dealt wondrously with you. My children will never again be ashamed.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Filial promise — the Father's people are named as his children.

Renderings:
• "My children" — ammi → "my children" per FHB-5.9-1 because filial context is established (children of Zion, sons and daughters)
• "be ashamed" — yevoshu (bosh root); literal English equivalent per FHB-7.10-1, not the older "put to shame"
• "who has dealt wondrously" — asher asah imakhem lehafli; preserved force of the Hebrew

Hebrew/Greek: ammi (my people) → "my children"
verses.footnote: In our Father's voice, his people are named as his children — the family name his heart has always carried.

verses.fhb_text: You will know that I am in the midst of Israel, that I am your Father, and there is no other. My children will never again be ashamed."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father's self-revelation — uniqueness and indwelling presence.

Renderings:
• "I am your Father" — ani YHWH eloheikhem; in YHWH's first-person speech the relational naming collapses to "your Father" rather than "our Father" since the Father cannot grammatically refer to himself as "our Father"
• "My children" — ammi per FHB-5.9-1
• Closing quote ends the YHWH speech that began at v.25

verses.fhb_text: "And afterward, I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh. Your sons and your daughters will prophesy; your old men will dream dreams; your young men will see visions.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father pours out his Spirit on his sons and daughters — Trinitarian filial promise quoted at Pentecost.

Renderings:
• "my Spirit" — ruchi; the Father naming the Holy Spirit as his own; preserved relational form per FHB-5.1.1-1
• "Your sons and your daughters" — bneikhem uvenoteikhem; explicit filial marker per FHB-3.1-1
• Semicolons preserve the parallel triad per FHB-9.4-2
verses.footnote: Our Father pours out his Spirit — the Holy Spirit — on his own sons and daughters; the Father, the Spirit, and the children are family.

verses.fhb_text: Even on the male and female servants, in those days, I will pour out my Spirit.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The outpouring extends to the lowest social rank — no one is excluded.

Renderings:
• "male and female servants" — ha'avadim ve-ha-shefachot; preserves the full social inclusion of the Hebrew

verses.fhb_text: I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Cosmic signs accompany the day of the LORD.

Renderings:
• "the heavens" — plural preserved per FHB-7.9-1
• Colon introduces the apposition of three signs

verses.fhb_text: The sun will be turned to darkness and the moon to blood, before the great and dreadful day of the LORD comes.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Cosmic darkening precedes the day of the LORD.

Renderings:
• "the day of the LORD" — fixed title preserved per FHB-1.2-1
• "will be turned" — modern future per FHB-9.10-1, replacing "shall be turned"

verses.fhb_text: And everyone who calls on the name of our Father will be saved. For on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem there will be deliverance, as our Father has said, and among the survivors whom he calls."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The chapter's culmination — salvation belongs to anyone who calls on the Father's name.

Renderings:
• "calls on the name of our Father" — yiqra beshem YHWH; the salvation formula in filial context
• Pronoun shift on third reference ("whom he calls") per FHB-9.2-1 to avoid over-naming
• "will be saved" — modern future per FHB-9.10-1
• Closing quote ends the YHWH speech that began at v.28
verses.footnote: Our Father saves anyone who calls on him by name — anyone who turns home as a child to a parent.

chapter_notes: Joel 2 establishes filial context throughout via three explicit markers: 'children of Zion' (v.23), ammi → 'my children' (vv.26-27 per FHB-5.9-1), and 'your sons and your daughters' (v.28). Per FHB-3.5.1-1 / FHB-3.5.3-1, 'our Father' rendering applies to all YHWH references in the chapter. 'Day of the LORD' preserved as fixed title at vv.1, 11, 31 per FHB-1.2-1 resolution order (overrides Father-rendering for this technical phrase). Locust/army dual imagery preserved throughout vv.1-11, 25 per FHB-5.11-1; four locust names (arbeh, yeleq, chasil, gazam) preserved at v.25. 'Where is their God?' (v.17) rendered with generic 'God' per FHB-1.2-1 speaker-context exception (the quoted speakers are pagan nations). Heart-language amplified for racham/chamal family terms per FHB-5.6.2-1: rachum → 'tender-hearted' (v.13), chamal → 'his heart went out' (v.18). Tirosh → 'new wine' (vv.19, 24) per FHB-4.5.1-1. Em-dash use limited to the appositional locust-name list at v.25, where the parenthetical genuinely earns its place per FHB-9.8-1. YHWH speeches structured: vv.12 (single-verse), vv.19-20, vv.25-27, vv.28-32; prophet voice in vv.21-24. In YHWH's first-person speech (v.27), 'I am your Father' renders YHWH-eloheikhem because the Father cannot grammatically refer to himself as 'our Father.'
